title: Burnham Takes Downing Street as SPR Buffer Runs Dry, AI Chip Crash Rocks Korea, and ECB Braces for September
subhead: Andy Burnham becomes UK prime minister promising cost-of-living relief, the US strategic petroleum reserve drawdown that cushioned the Iran war's oil shock is set to expire, South Korea's KOSPI plunges 8.8% in an AI-driven leverage unwinding, and the ECB holds rates this week but telegraphs a September hike as Brent claws back above $90.

sections.heading: Burnham Enters Downing Street Pledging Cost-of-Living Focus, Scraps Digital ID
sections.body:
Andy Burnham becomes the UK's seventh prime minister in just over a decade on Monday, moving into 10 Downing Street after a rapid ascent that saw him win a parliamentary seat in a special election last month and then take the Labour leadership on Friday. According to AP, Burnham arrives with 'a wave of enthusiasm behind him and a mountain of challenges ahead,' including a sluggish economy, overstretched public services, and wars in Ukraine and the Middle East.

One of his first acts will be to scrap the government's digital ID scheme, which had been estimated to cost £1.8 billion ($2.4 billion) between financial years 2026-27 and 2028-29. His spokesperson said all resources previously allocated to the scheme would be redirected toward cost-of-living priorities, according to CNBC. Deputy leader Lucy Powell described the move as 'one small example of reprioritizing.'

Sterling hovered near a two-month high against the dollar last week, according to Reuters, as fiscal concerns from the Starmer era eased. Burnham's team has committed to honoring existing North Sea oil and gas licenses, and Trump claimed on Truth Social that 'the people of Aberdeen are dancing in the streets' because Burnham 'will be opening up, all the way, the invaluable North Sea Oil.' Powell pushed back on this, saying Burnham would stick to the 2024 manifesto position of no new licenses but honoring existing ones.
sections.whyItMatters: Burnham's immediate pivot from digital infrastructure to cost-of-living relief signals where the political pressure is in the UK. The North Sea commitment matters for energy markets — maintaining existing licenses means continued production, but no new licenses signals a longer-term wind-down. Sterling's two-month high reflects relief at Starmer's departure, but the honeymoon will be short given the economic headwinds from oil prices and inflation.

sections.heading: Brent Breaks $90 Again as Hormuz Traffic Dwindles and SPR Buffer Nears Expiry
sections.body:
Brent crude surged past $90 per barrel on Monday, climbing more than 3% as the US-Iran conflict entered its ninth consecutive night of strikes. LSEG data cited by ING shows that only two outbound oil tankers transited the Strait of Hormuz over the weekend, with no inbound traffic — flows are 'essentially back to where they were before the Memorandum of Understanding,' according to ING's commodity analysts. A third US service member was killed in Iraq, and Iran's IRGC reported that two oil tankers exploded after hitting mines in the strait.

The critical backdrop: the coordinated release of 172 million barrels from the US Strategic Petroleum Reserve — structured as an exchange rather than a pure drawdown — is set to cease around the end of this month, according to ING. This release, alongside contributions from 31 other IEA member countries totaling 400 million barrels, has been the primary buffer cushioning the oil market from the Hormuz disruption. The IMF has warned that the factors preventing a larger oil spike — strategic reserve releases, increased non-Gulf production, and subdued Chinese demand — are becoming exhausted.

Iran has also reportedly told Houthi forces in Yemen to shut the Bab el-Mandeb Strait if the US attacks Iranian power infrastructure. This would close the Red Sea route that Saudi Arabia has relied on to bypass Hormuz during the war, making even Saudi oil exports to Asia lengthier and costlier.
sections.whyItMatters: The SPR drawdown ending this month removes the single biggest cushion between the global economy and a full-blown oil supply crisis. With only two tankers transiting Hormuz over the weekend and the Bab el-Mandeb now under threat, the physical oil market is operating on fumes. If the US extends the SPR exchange, it buys time but deepens the eventual refill challenge. If it doesn't, $90 Brent may look like a floor, not a ceiling.

sections.heading: Fed Hawks Circle as Warsh Signals No Tolerance for Sticky Prices, Markets See 76% Hike Odds
sections.body:
Fed Chair Kevin Warsh used his Capitol Hill testimony last week to deliver an unambiguous message: 'My commitment to you is to take sticky prices and to unstick them.' According to The Star/Bloomberg, Warsh stopped short of explicitly calling for a rate hike, but the tone was striking for a chair appointed by Trump, who had made clear he wanted lower rates.

Vice Chair Philip Jefferson went further, saying the central bank could consider raising rates if inflation doesn't cool soon. Governor Lisa Cook said she's prepared to act but has time. Dallas Fed President Lorie Logan called for higher rates, while Governor Christopher Waller and Cleveland President Beth Hammack warned there may be a case to raise rates. Hammack said businesses have told her for the first time in her tenure that the Fed needs to act.

Market positioning has shifted accordingly: year-end Fed hike probability has eased to 76% from 85% a week earlier, according to IG Markets, after June CPI fell 0.4% month-on-month (pulling annual rate to 3.5% against 3.8% consensus) and PPI slipped 0.3%. But the resurgent oil price threatens to reverse that disinflationary signal. The Fed's July 28-29 meeting is expected to hold rates steady, but at least three officials will vote who have publicly signaled hawkish leanings.
sections.whyItMatters: Warsh is testing a novel proposition: can a Trump-appointed Fed chair credibly threaten rate hikes while the White House wants cuts? The June CPI and PPI data gave hawks a temporary problem — but Brent above $90 gives them ammunition. With three hawkish voters on the July FOMC and a September decision still wide open, the 'family fight' Warsh has advocated could produce the first real dissent cluster of his tenure.

sections.heading: AI Chip Crash Wipes Out Korea's KOSPI as Leverage Unwinds
sections.body:
South Korea's KOSPI plunged 8.8% last week in an AI-driven leverage unwinding that hit global chip stocks. SK Hynix's Nasdaq debut failed to lift sentiment, with the stock diving 16%, while Samsung Electronics fell 11% amid a broader chip sell-off. Margin calls on leveraged positions, compounded by new curbs on single-stock leveraged ETFs, worsened the rout, according to IG Markets.

The Philadelphia Semiconductor Index (SOX) entered bear market territory, down 20.3% from its June record high. TSMC raised its capex guidance to $60-64 billion for 2026 from $52-56 billion, but investors grew skeptical that such spending would translate into commensurate profits. SanDisk dived 29.3% — the S&P 500's worst performer. The Nasdaq 100 fell 4.1% on the week.

A Chinese AI startup Moonshot launched its Kimi K3 model, reportedly rivaling leading US models at a fraction of the cost, evoking comparisons to the 2025 DeepSeek moment. This triggered further sell-offs in local rivals Zhipu (-32.5%) and MiniMax (-19.6%), and dragged SMIC down 15% and Lenovo 12.5%.
sections.whyItMatters: The Korea crash is a leverage problem masquerading as an AI problem. The KOSPI's 8.8% single-week decline was driven by margin calls and ETF restrictions, not a fundamental revaluation of chip demand. But the underlying question — whether the $60-64 billion in annual capex can generate returns — is the defining question for global markets. If the answer is no, the chip bear market is just beginning. If Kimi K3 is truly a DeepSeek moment, it means the cost of AI compute falls faster, compressing the revenue case for the very companies spending the most.

sections.heading: ECB Set to Hold Rates Thursday, With September Hike on the Table as Brent Surges
sections.body:
The European Central Bank is expected to keep rates unchanged at 2.25% when it meets on Thursday, July 23, according to multiple analysts and the IG Markets weekly outlook. But the hold comes with a clear warning: the September 10 meeting remains live for a second rate hike.

Bundesbank President Joachim Nagel set the tone last Wednesday, saying current rates are 'at an appropriate level' but adding: 'The renewed outbreak of military conflict in the Middle East and the fresh rise in oil prices underscore that the situation remains extremely volatile and uncertainty is correspondingly high. It remains appropriate to react with caution, but to act decisively if necessary.' The Indicator (Greece) reports that ECB policy decisions are now tied primarily to 'the trajectory of the war in Iran and how long the Strait of Hormuz will remain closed.'

Eurozone inflation fell to 2.8% in June from 3.2% in May, and Q2 GDP data due July 30 will be critical. The eurozone economy contracted 0.2% in Q1 (excluding Ireland, it grew 0.1%). EU gas storage is less than 54% full versus 64% last year and a 5-year average of 69%, making Europe particularly vulnerable to continued LNG supply disruption from the Gulf. European TTF gas prices were up more than 5% on Monday, breaking above EUR60/MWh.
sections.whyItMatters: The ECB is in a holding pattern, but the hold is conditional. Nagel's language — 'react with caution, but act decisively if necessary' — is the textbook formulation for a central bank that wants to keep a September hike live without spooking markets in July. The 54% EU gas storage level versus 69% five-year average is the most alarming number in this brief: Europe enters the heating season storage gap with the prospect of LNG disruption from both Hormuz and Bab el-Mandeb. That gap can't be closed by monetary policy, but it will force the ECB's hand on rates.

sections.heading: China's Q2 GDP Slows to 4.3%, Missing Target, as Stimulus Stays Calibrated
sections.body:
China's annual economic growth slowed to 4.3% in the second quarter, official data showed on Wednesday, missing analysts' expectations and falling below the lower end of the government's 4.5-5% full-year target, according to Reuters. Retail sales rose just 1%, and property investment fell 18% year-on-year in the first half, deepening the sector's crisis.

Beijing's response has been deliberately restrained. According to Reuters, economists and one government adviser said China can stabilize growth by accelerating already-budgeted national infrastructure investment projects, reducing the likelihood of large-scale fiscal stimulus. Markets are awaiting signals from the Politburo's expected meeting later this month.

The 4.3% growth figure masks a sharp divergence: resilient exports have been the primary support, but weak domestic demand and the oil shock from the Iran war are dragging on the consumption side. The Hang Seng rose 1.6% last week on AI optimism — Apple received regulatory approval to launch Apple Intelligence using Alibaba and Baidu technology — but the broader picture is one of a government choosing targeted infrastructure spending over broad stimulus.
sections.whyItMatters: China at 4.3% growth is not a crisis, but it is a problem for a global economy that has been counting on Chinese demand as a partial offset to the oil shock. Beijing's refusal to deploy large-scale stimulus means the oil demand destruction from slower Chinese growth will persist, providing some downward pressure on crude — but also reducing the global growth buffer at exactly the moment when Hormuz disruptions are removing supply.

sections.heading: India's Rupee Nears Record Low as Oil Pressure Mounts on RBI
sections.body:
The Indian rupee closed at 96.28 per dollar on Friday, down about 1% on the week — its steepest drop since May, when the currency hit its all-time low of 96.96, according to Business Standard. Traders expect the rupee to break below the psychologically important 96 level this week unless the Reserve Bank of India intervenes firmly.

The pressure is straightforward: India is one of the world's largest oil importers, and the Hormuz disruption is hitting its currency and inflation simultaneously. India's retail inflation crossed the RBI's 4% target in June, with some brokerages trimming rate hike projections while Capital Economics and ICICI Securities Primary Dealership still see hikes this year. The RBI's challenge is that defending the rupee requires tightening, but tightening into an oil-driven inflation shock risks strangling growth.

Foreign investors have net bought $2.3 billion of local stocks and bonds over July so far, and $4.3 billion of bonds over the past seven weeks. The 10-year benchmark yield ended Friday at 6.78%, up 7 basis points on the week, with traders watching for a Bloomberg Global Aggregate Index inclusion decision that could drive further inflows.
sections.whyItMatters: India is the canary in the coal mine for large oil-importing emerging markets. At 96.28, the rupee is a whisker from its all-time low, and the RBI's defense will drain reserves at exactly the moment when the oil import bill is surging. If Brent crosses $95 — as traders warn — the rupee will likely set new lows, forcing the RBI into an unenviable choice between reserve depletion and imported inflation.

bottomLine: Three forces collided this weekend: the physical oil market is losing its buffer as the SPR drawdown expires and Hormuz traffic dwindles to a trickle, AI chip stocks are experiencing a leverage-driven crash that has wiped 8.8% off Korea's KOSPI, and two major central banks — the ECB and the Fed — are holding rates this week but telegraphing that September could bring hikes if oil stays elevated. Andy Burnham's arrival in Downing Street matters for the UK but is a sideshow to the larger story: the world economy is heading into August without the 400-million-barrel IEA cushion that kept oil below $90 through the spring, and neither the ceasefire nor the SPR release that brought Brent down to $72 is coming back anytime soon.
sources: AP, CNBC, Reuters, Business Standard, ING, The Star/Bloomberg, IG Markets, Daily Sabah, The Indicator (Greece), Forbes, Seeking Alpha, Winnipeg Free Press/AP, NYT (live updates), MSN, CBS News, Singapore Business Times, Korean media via IG Markets
